---
format: revealjs
date: 2026-03-18
image: /images/17-control-plane.png
title-slide-attributes:
    data-background-image: /images/17-control-plane.png
    data-background-opacity: "0.3"
---

## Discussion {data-background-image="/images/18.png" data-background-opacity="0.1"}

- [These smart beds began roasting their owners](https://www.pcworld.com/article/2948826/these-smart-beds-began-roasting-their-owners-during-aws-outage.html)
    - What went wrong?
    - _Works as intended_ or _design flaw_?
    - What could we, the consumers, do differently?
- [Read more...](https://aws.amazon.com/message/101925/)

## Lesson Objectives {data-background-image="/images/18.png" data-background-opacity="0.1"}

1.	_Autonomous System_ and its role in network routing; understand boundaries and interior routing
2. Intra-AS: OSPF's use of LSAs for scalable routing
3. Inter-AS:  BGP as the primary protocol; exchanging routing information between different AS
4. Different types of BGP peering relationships, _eBGP_ and _iBGP_, and their significance in inter-AS communication.

### Textbook Readings {data-background-image="/images/18.png" data-background-opacity="0.1"}

- $5.3$ Intra-AS Routing in the Internet: OSPF (Open Shortest Path First)
- $5.4$ Routing Among the ISPs: BGP

::: {.notes}
- Scale + autonomy: billions of endpoints and independent administrations.
- “Shortest path” (OSPF) vs **policy-chosen** path (BGP)
- OSPF **area scope** of LSAs & role of **ABR** vs **ASBR**
- eBGP vs iBGP: who peers with whom?
- “Hot potato”: locally optimal ≠ globally optimal
- We’ll use small config/packet snippets to ground concepts 🧩
- OSPF ≈ performance within an admin domain
- BGP ≈ policy across domains
:::

## Recap {data-background-image="/images/18.png" data-background-opacity="0.1"}

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
### Link State Routing {data-background-image="/images/18.png" data-background-opacity="0.1"}

- Routers flood LSAs
- Global knowledge of network topology
- Dijkstra's algorithm to compute shortest paths
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
### Distance Vector {data-background-image="/images/18.png" data-background-opacity="0.1"}

- Share information with _neighbors_ only
- Iterative updates!
- Loops possible until convergence
:::
::::

::: {.notes}
- Which layer?
- What is the main function of this layer?
- What two planes exist at this layer? How are they different?
- What protocols did we learn so far at this layer?
:::

# Autonomous Systems (AS) {data-background-image="/images/18.png" data-background-opacity="0.1"}

## Why Autonomous Systems? {data-background-image="/images/18.png" data-background-opacity="0.1"}

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="60%"}
- How big is the Internet? 🌍
- What if every router shared full routing state? 🤯
- Could we group certain routers?
- And then share only reachability between groups?
- [Autonomous Systems (AS) to the rescue!](https://asrank.caida.org/)
:::
::: {.column width="40%"}
![](https://www.caida.org/projects/as-core/pics/2020/ascore-2020-ipv4-standalone.png)
:::
::::

::: {.notes}
- Cities = ASes; local roads (intra-AS); highways (inter-AS)
- Cities: local control, highways: agreed-upon rules.
- Highways: only share which cities are reachable, not every street.
- can’t store all destinations; exchanging full tables would swamp links.
- administrative control: each AS sets its own policies.
:::

## What is an AS? {data-background-image="/images/18.png" data-background-opacity="0.1"}

- A network (or set) under **single administrative control** 🏢
- Clear borders at edge routers; has an **AS number (ASN)**
- Goals: scalability, policy independence, fault containment
- What is your current AS?
- Which AS is the biggest? How many addresses does it cover?

::: {.notes}
* If students ask: 32-bit ASNs exist; not needed for this lesson’s depth.
:::

## Intra-AS vs Inter-AS Responsibilities {data-background-image="/images/18.png" data-background-opacity="0.1"}

* **Intra-AS:** pick efficient internal paths; optimize performance/availability
* **Inter-AS:** exchange **reachability** & enforce policy/economics 💸
* Different metrics, objectives, and protocols by design

::: {.notes}
* Plant the seed: intra-AS uses cost-like metrics; inter-AS uses attributes & policy.
:::

# Intra-AS Routing Protocols {data-background-image="/images/18.png" data-background-opacity="0.1"}

## Intra-AS Protocols {data-background-image="/images/18.png" data-background-opacity="0.1"}

* **RIP** (Distance Vector), **OSPF** (Link State), **IS-IS** (similar LS)
* **EIGRP** (DV-based; historically proprietary)
* **OSPF** is highly scalable, feature-rich

::: {.notes}
* Quick taxonomy; don’t deep-dive RIP/EIGRP—just context.
:::

## OSPF Core Mechanics {data-background-image="/images/18.png" data-background-opacity="0.1"}

* Flood **link-state** info (LSAs) to build a shared topology map
* Run **Dijkstra** to compute shortest paths → forwarding table
* Supports message **authentication** 🔐

::: {.notes}
* Stress “same view of the area’s topology” → consistent shortest paths.
:::

## OSPF Hierarchy & Areas {data-background-image="/images/18.png" data-background-opacity="0.1"}

* **Area 0 (backbone)** connects all other areas
* **Internal routers**: entirely inside one area
* **ABR**: between areas; **summarizes** & advertises to backbone
* **ASBR**: connects to other ASes; injects **external** routes
* Flooding stays **within an area**; summaries cross areas

::: {.notes}
* Key mental model: full knowledge of own area; only “direction/cost” to others.
:::

# Inter-AS Routing Protocols {data-background-image="/images/18.png" data-background-opacity="0.1"}

## What protocols exist? {data-background-image="/images/18.png" data-background-opacity="0.1"}

- EGP (Exterior Gateway Protocol)
- IDRP (Inter-Domain Routing Protocol)
- NIRA (Next Internet Routing Architecture)
- RIFT (Routing in Fat Trees)
- LISP (Locator/ID Separation Protocol)
- __BGP (Border Gateway Protocol)__

## Why BGP? {data-background-image="/images/18.png" data-background-opacity="0.1"}

* **Path-vector**: “Here I am, who I can reach, and how” 🧭
* Prioritizes **policy** & business relationships over pure performance
* The **only** inter-AS routing protocol in practice

## BGP Basics {data-background-image="/images/18.png" data-background-opacity="0.1"}

- Peers exchange reachability info (prefixes) + attributes
- Uses TCP (port 179) for reliable transport

![Textbook 5.2](http://gaia.cs.umass.edu/kurose_ross/LMS/images/Chapter_5_KC/ebgp_ibgp.jpg)

## eBGP {data-background-image="/images/18.png" data-background-opacity="0.1"}

- Peers between different autonomous systems
- Only advertises the single best path per prefix to neighbors
- Path-vector: advertises prefixes (NLRI) with attributes (AS-PATH, NEXT-HOP)
- Loop prevention: reject routes that contain your own ASN in AS-PATH
- Policy-driven: import/export filters decide what to learn/advertise; avoid unintended transit
- Edge learns from neighbors via eBGP, then distributes internally via iBGP

## iBGP {data-background-image="/images/18.png" data-background-opacity="0.1"}

- distribute external routes internally
- spread exits - reachability!
- **does not** change AS-PATH

::: {.notes}
* Policy caution: advertising a route can make you a **transit** path.
:::

## What exactly is being advertised? {data-background-image="/images/18.png" data-background-opacity="0.1"}

* __Prefix (NLRI)__: destination network (CIDR)
* __AS-PATH__: sequence of ASNs traversed to reach the prefix
* __NEXT-HOP__: IP of the border router to reach the next AS

![Textbook 5.2](http://gaia.cs.umass.edu/kurose_ross/LMS/images/Chapter_5_KC/ebgp_ibgp.jpg)

::: {.notes}
* Keep center stage on AS-PATH & NEXT-HOP; mention others as policy levers.
:::

::: {.notes}
* Connect to real-world economics: peering vs transit relationships.
:::

## Populating Forwarding Tables: Hot Potato {data-background-image="/images/18.png" data-background-opacity="0.1"}

* Exit the AS at the **closest** egress router 🥔🔥
* Minimizes internal cost; may increase total path length
* Chosen via attribute ordering + local policy (e.g., LOCAL_PREF, AS-PATH)

![Textbook 5.2](http://gaia.cs.umass.edu/kurose_ross/LMS/images/Chapter_5_KC/ebgp_ibgp.jpg)

::: {.notes}
* Contrast with “cold potato” (carry further internally)—policy/business choice.
:::

## Intra-AS vs Inter-AS {data-background-image="/images/18.png" data-background-opacity="0.1"}

* **Policy:** core in inter-AS; limited inside an AS
* **Scale:** OSPF hierarchy within AS; BGP summarization between ASes
* **Performance:** intra-AS optimizes metrics; inter-AS prioritizes policy

::: {.notes}
* Summarize all three axes: policy, scale, performance.
:::

# Review {data-background-image="/images/18.png" data-background-opacity="0.1"}

## OSPF LSAs always flood across the entire AS (all areas). {data-background-image="/images/18.png" data-background-opacity="0.1"}

A) True
A) False

::: {.notes}
* **Answer:** False. Most LSAs are scoped to an **area**; ABRs summarize across areas.
:::

## Which statement about **iBGP** is correct? {data-background-image="/images/18.png" data-background-opacity="0.1"}

A) It carries routes between different ASes
A) It modifies AS-PATH to add the local AS
A) It distributes external reachability **within** an AS
A) It replaces OSPF for intra-AS forwarding

::: {.notes}
* **Answer:** It distributes external reachability within an AS. iBGP does not change AS-PATH and does not replace OSPF’s role in forwarding.
:::

## The main motivation for “hot potato” routing is to: {data-background-image="/images/18.png" data-background-opacity="0.1"}

A) Minimize end-to-end Internet latency
A) Minimize **internal** AS cost to the nearest exit
A) Satisfy legal requirements
A) Evenly balance load across all links

::: {.notes}
* **Answer:** Minimize **internal** AS cost to the nearest exit.
:::

## Match the protocol model: {data-background-image="/images/18.png" data-background-opacity="0.1"}

A) OSPF — distance vector; BGP — link state
A) OSPF — link state; BGP — path vector
A) OSPF — flooding forbidden; BGP — flooding required
A) OSPF — policy-based; BGP — performance-based

::: {.notes}
* **Answer:** OSPF — link state; BGP — path vector.
:::

## Why might an AS choose **not** to advertise a learned route to a neighbor? {data-background-image="/images/18.png" data-background-opacity="0.1"}

A) It breaks the TCP three-way handshake
A) Privacy/trust/policy concerns or to avoid becoming transit
A) iBGP forbids external advertisement
A) OSPF area mismatch

::: {.notes}
* **Answer:** Privacy/trust/policy concerns or to avoid becoming a transit path.
:::

## Which BGP attribute tells a router where to send packets to reach the next AS? {data-background-image="/images/18.png" data-background-opacity="0.1"}

A) AS-PATH
A) NEXT-HOP
A) LOCAL_PREF
A) MED

::: {.notes}
* **Answer:** **NEXT-HOP**. AS-PATH lists ASNs; LOCAL_PREF/MED are policy signals.
:::

## Key Takeaways {data-background-image="/images/18.png" data-background-opacity="0.1"}

* The Internet scales by grouping routers into **ASes** with local control
* **OSPF** (intra-AS): link-state + Dijkstra; LSAs scoped by **area**; ABR/ASBR roles; Area 0 backbone
* **BGP** (inter-AS): path-vector + **policy**; eBGP across ASes, iBGP inside an AS
* Key attributes: **AS-PATH** and **NEXT-HOP**; selective advertisement is normal
* **Hot potato**: exit at nearest egress to minimize internal cost—even if globally longer

## [Can BGP be hijacked?](https://www.cloudflare.com/learning/security/glossary/bgp-hijacking/) {data-background-image="/images/18.png" data-background-opacity="0.1"}

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="60%"}
- [Pakistan Telecom (2008)](https://www.ripe.net/about-us/news/youtube-hijacking-a-ripe-ncc-ris-case-study/)
- [Amazon Route53 (2018)](https://blog.cloudflare.com/bgp-leaks-and-crypto-currencies/)
- [KLAYswap (2022)](https://blog.citp.princeton.edu/2022/03/09/attackers-exploit-fundamental-flaw-in-the-webs-security-to-steal-2-million-in-cryptocurrency/)
- [Russia (2023)](https://www.bgpmon.net/popular-destinations-rerouted-to-russia/)
- [U.S. research & education regional network (2024)](https://internet2.edu/what-the-research-education-community-learned-from-three-impactful-routing-security-incidents-in-2024/)
- [Cloudflare (2025)](https://blog.cloudflare.com/cloudflare-1-1-1-1-incident-on-july-14-2025/)
:::
::: {.column width="40%"}
- What went wrong?
- Was it accidental or malicious?
- What was the intended effect?
- What was the impact? Who suffered?
- Who benefited?
:::
::::
